payload.result:
{"results": [{"chunk_id": "h20_export_restriction::000", "title": "H20 China export restriction and writedown", "published_at": "2025-04-16", "score": 10.212, "excerpt": "# H20 China export restriction and writedown

NVIDIA disclosed in an 8-K filed after the close on April 15 that the U.S. government informed the company on April 9 that a license is now required to export its H20 integrated circuits — and any products achieving the H20's memory bandwidth or interconnect bandwidth — to China (including Hong Kong and Macau) and D:5 countries. On April 14, the government told NVIDIA the license requirement will be in effect for the indefinite future. The stated rationale is the risk that the products may be used in, or diverted to, supercomputers in China.

The financial hit is concrete: NVIDIA said first-quarter fiscal 2026 results (quarter ending April 27) ar..."}, {"chunk_id": "ai_capex_skeptic::001", "title": "The bear case: AI capex digestion risk", "published_at": "2025-05-10", "score": 7.511, "excerpt": "Third, the accounting. Massed GPU fleets are depreciating assets on five-to-six-year schedules against still-thin AI application revenue. Every quarter of elevated capex raises the future earnings hurdle for the buyers — a treadmill that ends either with AI monetization arriving on time, or with a capex digestion phase like 2019's cloud pause.

Add the idiosyncratic risks: the H20 ban just removed most of NVIDIA's China data-center business, and the 10-K shows striking customer concentration, with individual direct customers repeatedly crossing 10% of revenue.

None of this says the May 28 print misses; the spring earnings round showed 2025 budgets intact. payload.result:
{"results": [{"chunk_id": "hyperscaler_capex::000", "title": "Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)", "published_at": "2025-05-02", "score": 19.556, "excerpt": "# Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)

The March-quarter earnings round from the big cloud buyers — Alphabet (April 24), Microsoft and Meta (April 30), and Amazon (May 1) — settled, at least for now, the question hanging over AI infrastructure names since the winter's data-center-lease scare: the 2025 budgets are intact, and one of them went up.

Meta raised its full-year 2025 capital-expenditure outlook to $64–72 billion, from $60–65 billion previously, citing additional data-center investments to support AI and higher expected costs of infrastructure hardware. Mark Zuckerberg framed the spend around AI now touching nearly every Meta product.

Alphabet reaffirmed its plan..."}, {"chunk_id": "ai_capex_skeptic::000", "title": "The bear case: AI capex digestion risk", "published_at": "2025-05-10", "score": 17.252, "excerpt": "# The bear case: AI capex digestion risk

The bull case for NVIDIA needs only one assumption: that hyperscaler capital spending keeps compounding. The bear case is that we are watching the top of an investment cycle, and the warning lights are already blinking.

Start with the lease checks. TD Cowen's supply-chain work found Microsoft walked away from data-center projects totaling roughly two gigawatts of capacity across the U.S. and Europe over six months — driven, in their read, by a decision not to support incremental OpenAI training workloads. problems: Claims c1 and c2 share the same 5-day horizon but are mutually inconsistent: c1 predicts NVDA closes at least +3% while c2 predicts the 5-day return lands between -8% and +2%, so the +3% threshold is outside c2's range and the two cannot both be correct.
